key_insight: Hierarchical granular metamaterials use architected grains, ordered packings, and programmed defects to boost impact energy absorption while lowering transmitted peak force.
engineering_relevance: The design offers a lightweight way to tune dissipation, stress routing, and even transport pathways independently for impact-resistant multifunctional components.
method: Hollow elliptical inclusions create lightweight grains with stronger lateral expansion under load.
Crystal-inspired packings and engineered tessellations recruit neighboring grains to spread deformation.
Functional gradients and defects program contact networks for deterministic mechanical and electrical routing.
evidence_signal: In situ nanomechanical tests, nonlinear computational models, and multiscale impact experiments show higher energy absorption per mass and lower peak transmitted force across materials and length scales.
watch_item: Performance depends on precisely controlled grain geometry and packing, so manufacturing variability could reduce the predicted dissipation and routing behavior.
